comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight has arrived, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 267,446th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.2 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week.  This was my fifty-fourth published book, the conclusion of The Weird of Hali. It had its genesis, in a certain sense, decades earlier, when I read Susan Cooper's The Dark is Rising series of fantasies for older children. The first four books in the sequence -- The Dark is Rising; Over Sea, Under Stone; Greenwitch; and The Grey King -- were first-rate, vivid fantasies that drew on British folklore and legend to chronicle a tale of warring magical forces in modern Britain. I adored them and read them over and over again.

Then came the final book in the sequence, Silver On The Tree, which I read once to my bitter disappointment and never touched again. The grand final struggle between the Light and the Dark reached its grand anticlimax in a scene just a few pages long, and then all the wizards and magical beings packed their bags and went away forever, leaving the other characters sitting in the prosaic modern world. Cooper might as well have said in so many words, "All right, children, playtime is over, now forget all about magic and wonder and go back to your boring lives." I never forgave her for that, and many years passed before I read any of her books again.

So when I found myself writing a story that in some ways approximated The Dark is Rising series -- well, as seen through a tentacular funhouse mirror -- I promised myself from the beginning that I wouldn't do the same thing to my readers. You'll notice on the cover the image of Great Cthulhi rising from the sea. In Arkham, the stars are right at last, and nothing will ever be the same again. Interested? You can get a copy here if you live in the US and here elsewhere.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed, and no further comments will be put through. See you next week!***

comments.title: Soul Crystals: The Republican Answer to Gulags?
comments.content:
Hi JMG, as a progressive I've been understandably dismayed by the election, but moreso by some social media posts I've seen which claim that Donald Trump has the ability to extract the souls of his critics and imprison them inside of crystals. In your experience, how likely is this? Is there some occult means by which Trump could capture my soul and confine it in a crystal, and if so how long could I be trapped in such a manner? For eternity? Are the means of doing this well known among magical practicioners, or is this a particular talent of Trump's? Please pardon my lack of familiarity with this topic, until recently I never considered the existence of magic in real life.

Also, is it possible to extract some form of power from the imprisoned souls? Could this be part of Trump's plan to make the US more energy-independent? I've had the persistent disturbing thought that he may use my soul as a power source for the US electrical grid and classify it as a "green technology" to avoid sanctions by the EU.

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Soul Crystals: The Republican Answer to Gulags?
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, the "souls trapped in crystals" thing is a joke based on the Elder Scrolls video game, not anything real. You might find this KnowYourMeme article useful.

Second, that's not the kind of thing that real magic can do. Magic was defined by Dion Fortune, one of the top practitioners in 20th century Britain, as "the art and science of causing changes in consciousness in accordance with will." The crap you see in Harry Potter movies, video games, etc.? That's not real magic. (The Harry Potter franchise has as much to do with real magic as Young Frankenstein has to do with real science.)

So I'd encourage you to relax. Trump isn't going to bind your soul into a crystal, or throw you into a gulag, or anything like that. Despite all the stuff that's been projected onto him by the fearful, he's just another politician, you know.

replies.title: Re: Soul Crystals: The Republican Answer to Gulags?
replies.content:
I had thought it was a Superman reference.

Is it possible to trap a mind virus 'in the crystals', at least? What about a manufactured luxury belief, such as those doozies produced at the Harvard University humanities department?

replies.title: Re: Soul Crystals: The Republican Answer to Gulags?
replies.content: Unfortunately not. Crystals don't work well as traps for much of anything -- well, other than the wallets of credulous New Agers, which they seem to attract and empty with remarkable regularity.

replies.title: Re: Soul Crystals: The Republican Answer to Gulags?
replies.content:
Well, per Fortune's definition, Trump seems rather apt at capturing the minds of his detractors and imprisoning those in a hall of mirrors, anxiety and despair; and which may involve crystals or not. Fortunately for the OP one can, at least theoretically, choose to not be triggered, ignore the troll and do something else with one's time and money.

Of course you will need to avoid needless exposure to whatever policies He might enact, but the same is true about every politician past, present and future. In that front, being collapse aware (use LESS, get ahead of the rush, there's no better future) has done much more to my peace of mind than any particular strategy I could have devised.

replies.title: Re: Soul Crystals: The Republican Answer to Gulags?
replies.content:
I really have to wonder if this is a troll question, especially with the "green technology" and "EU Sanctions" bit at the end.

If not, my apologies to the OP - it can definitely be hard to figure out where to draw the line on what's believable when your previous ways of determining that have been scrapped.

replies.title: Re: Soul Crystals: The Republican Answer to Gulags?
replies.content: Oh, I definitely considered that possibility, but there are some people who believe such things and I figured a straight answer wouldn't be wasted.

replies.title: Re: Soul Crystals: The Republican Answer to Gulags?
replies.content:
This wisdom matches my own! I draw a parallel to your Tartary article. You made your own beliefs clear, but had enough respect to those who didn't share your beliefs to "trigger warn" them.

In my experience, straight answers are never wasted. Take your conversation partner to their logical extreme, and extend compassion once they get there. If you understand them, and if you drop the expectation that they'll be exactly on your page once all is done (or, more radically, you'll learn from them,) you can reach amazing heights.

replies.title: Re: Soul Crystals: The Republican Answer to Gulags?
replies.content:
My first reaction was, "wait, is this a troll or a daft boomer?" Honestly, sometimes it's difficult to differentiate the two these days.

Not to dunk on boomers too much, but I think our gracious host and Scott Adams (of Dilbert comics fame) are the only boomers in existence who actually understand memes. The rest of them pearl-clutch at just about anything people under the age of 60 find funny.

replies.title: Re: Soul Crystals: The Republican Answer to Gulags?
replies.content: I'm honored!

comments.title: Sphere of Protection
comments.content:
I have recently restarted basic ritual work again, after a long break caused by a breakdown of sorts. I had been practicing the druid form of the LBRP for a number of years, but hadn't really progressed to the middle pillar and its variants,so I wonder if I just bluntly banished too much.

Anyway, I had a fairly severe attack of the watcher, or my own shadow, whatever it may have been, so I stopped.

Fast forward to the end of the covid insanity, and whatever that cosmic eclipse was, and I am pleasantly surprised with the different feeling of the SoP.

The LBRP is more powerful up front, but it certainly feels spookier, if that's the right way to put it. Different energies I guess.

The SoP is remarkably different, it resembles to me, not much more than a good walk in the outdoors.

I wonder if that's what others find?

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection
replies.content: Most people I know of who've tried the SoP find it gentler and more balanced. That doesn't make it weak -- it's a fine protective ritual among other things -- but it's subtler, and most people find it takes them more repetitions to get it up to full power. For whatever this is worth, it's my daily protective ritual these days.

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection
replies.content:
Hi JMG,
I have used the SOP for the past two days to make a vessel to pray to Bastet. My older cat is very upset that we have a kitten and has started leaving for days at a time. Each time I prayed to Bastet within the SP, my cat turned up within 3 hours. I have added Bastet to my regular pantheon.

My older cat actually seemed happy to see us and let me give him a good brushing as well as a large bowl of fresh meat.

Maxine

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection
replies.content: Glad to hear it.

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection
replies.content:
I find the SoP much more congenial than the LBRP was when I did that. It did seem to take awhile to build up, but it is very robust and flexible.

Dijon Demented Goat

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection
replies.content:
I haven't tried the LBRP, but based on my experience with the SoP, I'd recommend to stick with it.

A good walk outdoors is a good comparison. The power in it isn't in the individual walk, but in the habit, and in its flexibility and exploratory potential.

Might be my personal bias, but I've had TSW experiences too quickly in my spiritual path, and have shied away from those. Those didn't seem like what benefits me.

comments.title: Will and causality...
comments.content:
Thank you for your response for my question on the dementia&dying stuff. A lot of my own intuition opened up after expressing the question. One snippet I got from some talk by Iain McGilchrist is that what if our consciousness has different phases that are not compatible, and not even conscious of each other, like water/ice/steam? This helps me in letting go of the idea that dementia is a punishment. And concerning skills to die: a lot hinges on what gets deeply entrained during life. So any consistent practice&ritual may give its richest fruits at the final stage of life. Getting out of the door/entering what is beyond in decency.

Now I have a new question. The brain functions in somewhat different way than the world outside. The cause and effect of the billiard ball-style world makes things appear predictable and we have reached a great level of control of the natural world through this mechanistic way of thinking. But the inner workings of the brain happen quite differently. There, a network of different points of activation creates images in consciousness. And these images are fairly individual. Some may get warm fuzzy feelings from Trump, some from Biden, for example. The states of consciousness are like layered sandwiches, that can contain stuff from emotions, body states etc. Psychology&marketing/propaganda kind of stuff - not that mechanistic. Right now, giant efforts are made to manhandle our consciousnesses into certain directions, that are not matching the cause-and-effect-word very well. Ukraine is winning - but it is shrinking, too. The green energy is gonna save us - don't hold your breath. So we are living in a time where consciousnesses are being changed in accordance with will. It is working, as we all know some who are firm believers of some strange theory of what will save us. Our venerable host mentioned somewhare, that the famous definition of magic does not specify whose will. This has been my stumbling stone for years, as I learned in my younger years, that getting what I strogly willed was possible, but also quite devastating. So I've been watching the world, and thinking: Whose will is it? And ultimately it is also a question of what affects what? We are, right now, well-positioned to destroy a lot of our civilization because of holding on to an idea of the opponent being the evillest evilness there ever was. Wrecking the "external" world for pictures in our minds. So, changes in consciousness in accordance with will are happening. Now we see, what horrible weapons unhinged wills are. And why it was a good idea to have some things esoteric and occult. But here we are, and I keep asking whose will is it? Is there something beyond the clash of wills? The buddhist oxherding pictures come to mind.
Kristiina, today, appropriately for the ponderous&heavy post, Dreary Vexatious Skull (the name in itself is an answer, so thank you, cosmic oom! I'll go look for some juicy liveliness now.)

replies.title: Re: Will and causality...
replies.content: These are excellent questions which, ultimately, each of us has to answer for ourselves. One hint which I hope you'll find useful, though, is that there are many wills at work and many consciousnesses being affected by them. William of Ockham was quite simply wrong -- the simplest answer is not the most accurate one!

replies.title: Re: Will and causality...
replies.content:
I'm a solipsist, and I JUST don't understand why everybody else isn't one as well...
There is a level of consciousness that exists under the layers you are talking about, and it's very difficult to get ahold of it, to perceive it. The way I started to get at it was to think of any of my thoughts as being like a bubble in a glass of champagne. As it rises up, it gets covered in images, words, and associated feelings, but before any of that happens it's coming from a level that, to our usual consciousness, is formless, tasteless, and therefore invisible. That's where science loses hold of things, because THAT level can't be found or weighed, or measured on a scale or EEG machine.
So we participate in a "universe" that is consensually constructed because it is the only way we can all function together "down here". So an early step is to gain control of *your* consciousness. THAT'S the first "change in consciousness in accordance with will" that happens under your direction. YOU take agency over what is going on in your consciousness, which means being aware of what thoughts are yours, what thoughts are being implanted in your mind by McDonalds while you sit passively in front of your television watching "Walker, Texas Ranger" reruns, and, much later, what thoughts are being implanted in your consciousness by psychic means. In between commercials and psychic methods falls what H. Spencer Lewis called "Mental Poisoning" meaning things that are suggested to you by subtle verbal or other still material cues by people you know, either intentionally or unintentionally. All of this is a long and drawn out struggle, just to gain control of your own inner mental borders. THEN there's the possibility of causing changes in the consciousnesses of others, but, with what you've learned wresting control of your own mind back from the control of others you've ceded it to, you might realize that it's not such a great idea to try those old Jedi mind tricks on other people. Karma is an absolute bitch about doing that willfully and with knowledge aforethought.
Sorry to ramble. Hope it was useful.

Good morning and thank you for hosting.

You’ve talked before about how the most effective use of magical practice is to create change within the self, rather than trying to force the world to change around you. I’ve taken on this philosophy in my approach to the art, but I find it challenging to determine the limits—often it seems as though magical practice seeks some kind of “true self” or higher genius, which seems to suggest that there is something like a predefined end state.

Are there certain aspects of the self that are pretty much set in stone and unlikely to change even if they’re in deep tension with the kind of life one wants to lead? On one hand limits of various kinds are foundational to reality (and sometimes accepting that certain things just aren’t going to happen is part of growing up) but on the other hand it’s easy to fall into the trap of false limitations created by one’s beliefs and state of consciousness, and I don’t really know how to distinguish them clearly.

comments.title: Freemasons and the Albert Pike letter
comments.content: I know you don't believe the Masons are a part of a conspiracy for world domination and evil, but what do you make of the Albert Pike letter which supposedly was to incite world war of total destruction, apostasy of Catholics which then enables the ascension of the Antichrist and worship of Lucifer?

replies.title: Re: Freemasons and the Albert Pike letter
replies.content:
That letter was originally forged by the practical joker and pornographer Gabriel Jogand-Pages under his pen name Leo Taxil, and published in his book Le Diable au XIXème Siècle (The Devil in the 19th Century) in 1896. It was then thoroughly rewritten by William Guy Carr (who inserted the stuff about the three world wars) and published in his book Pawns in the Game in 1958. That is to say, it's a complete fake. You might find this essay useful in that regard.

As far as Freemasonry goes, I'd like to remind you that George Washington, Benjamin Franklin, and Paul Revere were all Freemasons, while Adolf Hitler, Vladimir Lenin, and the Ayatollah Khomeini all banned Freemasonry as soon as they took power. Whose side would you rather be on?

replies.title: Re: Freemasons and the Albert Pike letter
replies.content: Thank you very much.  You are a fountain of knowledge.

When he was in Iran, they still had that system where you sign your name on library cards. Just about every single occult book he took out from the libraries (I think university library, but not sure) had the Ayatollah's name as a previous borrower (obviously this was before he came into power)

comments.title: Monks Mediating Blessings

Since the topic of blessing has come up a few times, I thought I would chime in with a few thoughts.

I have experienced the benefits of blessings offered by people in this community. Specifically Milkyway and Kyle. I want to thank them for their efforts.

I saw something over the weekend that got me thinking. It was a story about a girl who wanted to join a nunnery. Her mom didn't want her to and then she did anyway. She promised to pray for her benefactor who helped change the moms mind.

Considering the blessings here, and monasticism in the past and future... one of the benefits of having monks and nuns and the like is that they will be praying and blessing for their communities on a regular basis. The rituals and other work they do will also benefit the general etheric and astral health of the area they are in, contributing to the community.

This is quiet work that goes on in the background, but work none the less, and the supportive role it gives to those in the community can't be discounted enough.

In terms of (de)industrialized(ing) world, I think part of the reason we have so many issues as we now do, is because there are fewer people praying (it seems) for each other on than their might have been in the past when the population was smaller and their were more monastic communities. At least here in the USA it seems like that is the case. I am sure we will see a return to a robust monasticism.

In the meantime it does seem that those of us called to Universal Monks, Nuns, or in a Priesthood / Mage capacity can do a lot of benefit to the world and those around us.

Peace.

comments.title: Musings
comments.content:
Hello Everyone,

When I was quite young, I perceived what I'll call 'flows' (for lack of a better understanding) between objects or occurrences--and not always in contemporary time. As a random example, one day while walking with my dad--I was six or seven--I asked him, 'if time is something we're moving through..' (I'd just been introduced to H.G. Wells) '...and maybe you can go back in time, does that mean that everything actually exists all at once, but just in different times?' (or words to that effect). Poor dad looked askance at me and suggested we get a nice cold soda.

When we'd travel on vacation, I'd be drawn to what I'd now call 'manifestations of deep time'--finding a fossil coral in an Illinois state park was very nearly an ecstatic experience and in my imagination the forested hills around me became the shores of an ancient sea. Not surprisingly, friends and neighbors didn't quite feel the same way ('oh, what a nice rock you've found!').

Over and over I'd faintly, yet unmistakably, feel the presence of 'something'--energies, or flows, or sacredness. In young adulthood the magic went away for some time, until in my early fifties it pressed in, demanding my attention once more, and more insistently than ever.

So I am a complete neophyte in exploration of the occult--and yet I hope that I now have the humility, and to some degree the wisdom, to pursue it in a beneficent, humble and grateful manner--which I could not have done when I was younger.

I look forward to the journey, and a special thanks to John Michael Greer for his books (of which I own a fair number) and for his insightful commentary--I've been following you, drawn by 'Peak Oil', since 2006.

Thanks so much!

replies.title: Re: Musings

comments.title: The De-colourizing of Society
comments.content:
As a person who grew up in the wall-to-wall kaleidoscope of colour that was 1960s North America, I have become increasingly alarmed about the trend of ‘banning’ colours in our society, from the perspective of mental health. I assume that there may be an occult dimension to all this. Allow me to elaborate. First, I noticed it with new cars: for the past decade or more the palette of colours for many models consists of white, black, and various shades of grey. Then came interior decorating: black and white renovated kitchens with metal-finish appliances became all the rage. But it hasn’t stopped there… in my city people have within the past two years been painting their red brick houses all white with black front door and window frames; or instead of white, some shade of grey. One house in my neighbourhood has been done over with black and a shade of grey that closely resembles November rain clouds: it looks like the Munsters would feel at home in it – ugh! And just this last weekend I was walking in a shopping mall, and I glanced at an H&M clothing store: not only were the walls and décor black, white and grey, but so were all the clothes in the store as far as the eye could see! Meanwhile, society has become polarized into diametrically opposed camps in so many different arenas, in which not a single view of the ‘opposite camp’ can be tolerated.

I see this ‘fatwa on colour’ as an unhealthy development which makes me yearn for the ‘60s and ‘70s when black and white were ‘endangered colours.’ I find it to be as harsh and dehumanizing as the brutalist architectural movement. What’s next: arresting people for displaying a splash of colour on their persons (hair colour exempted, perhaps)? So much for ‘diversity’, eh?

Any occult observations on this phenomenon would be greatly appreciated!

Ron M

comments.title: On mental images
comments.content:
This may not be directly related to magic but it plagues my visualization practices.

I have trouble with “misbehaving” mental creations. As an example, I will create an image in my mind of a large chest that I can place items into. But when I try to close and lock it, it bursts open. I try a heavier lid but it too gets shaken off.
Another example, a simple rotating sphere. The exercise will start out okay but I’ll lose control and it moves about under its own accord.

Is this something you’ve dealt with or have insights into how to master these objects?

replies.title: Re: On mental images
replies.content:
It's something everybody deals with. Have you done any work yet with scrying, aka active imagination? That's a practice where you encourage the imagery to take its own course.

Use the difficulties as a source of insight. If the chest you imagine keeps on popping open, that's a message. What are you trying to stash away that won't stay put, and why? Are you of two minds about putting it there? Discursive meditation is one good way to unpack such things.

replies.title: Re: The Cryptogram as a Factor in Symbolic Philosophy
replies.content:
Not JMG, but if you want to play around with this more, a few ideas to get you started:

1) The technique in the post you mentioned falls under the field of "steganography," which is hiding your secret message inside a seemingly normal piece of writing (as opposed to something that looks like a jumble of random letters, which is a cypher, or a series of words with no apparent meaning, which might be a code).

2) Most, if not all, non-mechanical methods of unraveling cryptography involve lots of guess and check. You have more things to guess if you learn more methods folks have tried in the past (like using the first letter of each paragraph, or of each sentence, or the third word of the fifth sentence of each paragraph, or whatever). Once you know what a single-substitution cipher is (you substitute one letter for another in a fixed way, such as "if the original message has an 'a', write an 'n' instead), you can look at some cipher text and start substituting a different letter for each instance of a letter in the cipher text (if you got lucky and guessed right for my example a moment ago, you might try substituting an 'a' everywhere you saw an 'n').

3) The guess-and-check is made more effective with the help of some pattern matching, some of which is more intuitive, but it also helps to know some of the patterns you might want to look for. For example, in English, the most commonly used letters are, in this order, ETAIN. So, if you had a cipher text where the most common letter was 'q,' you might try substituting an 'e' everywhere there's a 'q,' and if the next most common letter is 'z,' you might put a 't' in place of each of those. Then, if you see a lot of 'tDe's floating around, you might guess that 'D' is standing for 'h,' spelling "the." Repeat this for a while, and you can fill in more and more gaps, like playing hangman.

4) Lastly, if you have some guess about what the enciphered text might be talking about, you can make some educated guesses about words that are likely to be included. For an occult text, you might expect that "spirit" would show up, so you might see if any of your guesses for possible ciphers happen to spit out "spirit" when you apply one of your hypotheses.

Anyhow, there's loads of stuff out there on this stuff, some folks do it for a hobby, like playing sudoku or doing crosswords. A nice, readable history of cryptography through the 90s is The Code Book by Simon Singh, and a fun novel that deals with the cutting edge of cryptology in WWII and in the 90s is Neal Stephenson's Cryptonomicon.

Happy hunting!
Jeff
